title: Big-O - how the work grows
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: Big-O describes how the work an algorithm does grows as the input (n) gets bigger - not the exact time, just the shape of the growth.
explanation: Big-O is the language for 'how does this scale?' We ignore constants and small stuff and ask: as n doubles, what happens? O(1) means the work doesn't grow at all (a hash lookup). O(n) means it grows in step with the input (one loop). O(n^2) means a loop inside a loop - it grows much faster. O(log n) - halving each step, like a binary search - barely grows at all. Picking the right structure is mostly about picking the better Big-O.

exercises.question: A single loop over n items is...
hints: Double the items, double the work.
It grows in step with n.

exercises.question: A loop inside a loop, each over n items, is...
hints: For each of the n outer steps, you do n inner steps.

exercises.question: Looking one key up in a hash map is...
hints: It jumps straight to the slot - no searching.
The size of the map doesn't matter.
